blurb: Fat little pea-sized beads strung along trailing threads, each with a translucent 'window' slit to let light in. The classic hanging succulent — and the plumper, rounder cousin of my String of Tears.

care.light: Bright, indirect light with a little gentle morning sun keeps the beads plump and tightly strung; harsh midday sun scorches them and deep shade leaves the strands sparse and stretched.
care.water: Soak-and-dry — the round beads store their own water, so I wait until they start to look a touch soft, then water and let it dry right out. Both extremes are bad: staying wet rots the shallow roots, bone-dry shrivels the pearls.
care.soil: A fast-draining cactus mix, or potting soil cut with plenty of sand or perlite and grit. A wide, shallow pot suits the shallow roots.
care.temperature: Happiest warm (70–80°F) through summer and a bit cooler (50–60°F) in winter; keep it above freezing.
care.dormancy: Active in the warmer months (spring through early autumn) and quieter in winter — I water sparingly, if at all, once the days shorten.
care.feeding: Modest appetite — a half-strength general-purpose liquid feed about once a month through the growing season is plenty.
care.potting: Lovely spilling from a raised shelf or hanging pot to show off the strands. The roots are shallow, so I only repot when it's truly crowded, always into something free-draining.
care.propagation: Very easy — take a 3–5 in stem cutting or lay a strand on the mix so the nodes touch soil; it roots in about a month. This one's my second try, replacing the string I lost to overwatering.
care.bloom: Small white, daisy-like flowers on short stalks in summer, with a surprising cinnamon scent — though it rarely blooms indoors.
care.troubles: Overwatering and the rot that follows are by far the main risk; the water-storing beads mean it forgives a missed watering far more than a soggy pot.
care.toxicity: Toxic to cats and dogs (and horses) if eaten — the sap carries pyrrolizidine alkaloids and can irritate skin too, so I keep it up high and out of reach.
